criteria: The agent has read the Introduction and the model overview (Fig. 1) completely to understand that CrysCo is a hybrid framework combining a structure-based GNN (CrysGNN) and a composition-based transformer (CoTAN) trained jointly, augmented with a transfer-learning variant (CrysCoT) for data-scarce mechanical properties.
The agent has read the 'Graph representation of crystal structure' subsection completely to understand the construction of the G8, L(G8), L(G8d), and L(G8a) graphs.
The agent has read the 'Model architecture for crystal structure' subsection, including Equations 1-6, to understand the EGAT node/edge update mechanism.
The agent has read the 'Human-knowledge representation of materials' and 'Transformer-based architecture for compositional features' subsections to understand the CoTAN pipeline.

The agent has read the 'Model training for both parallel architectures' and 'Transfer learning for data-scarce material properties' subsections to understand the training protocol and the CrysCoT fine-tuning scheme.
The agent has read Table 1 and the Dataset subsection to understand the eight MP21 property datasets used for benchmarking.
The agent has read Table 2 and the accompanying discussion to understand CrysCo's comparative performance against MEGNet, CGCNN, SchNet, ALIGNN, Matformer, and DeeperGATGNN.
The agent has read Table 3 and Figure 2 to understand the EGAT layer-count ablation study and its accuracy/efficiency trade-off.
The agent has read the Interpolation analysis, Extrapolation analysis, and Effect of dataset size on model convergence subsections to understand the transfer-learning evaluation protocol.

The agent has read the 'Elemental contributions to thermodynamic stability' and 'Filling-in elastic data missing from the Materials Project database' subsections to understand the interpretability and data-imputation analyses.
The plan includes building two parallel networks -- a structure-based CrysGNN and a composition-based CoTAN -- trained jointly in a single hybrid model (CrysCo).
The plan includes constructing the atomic graph G8 using an 8 Angstrom cutoff radius, where any pair of atoms within that radius is connected by an edge, instead of a minimally-connected graph.
The plan includes constructing the line graph L(G8), whose nodes represent bond distances and whose edges represent bond angles.
The plan includes constructing the dihedral graph L(G8d), whose edges represent dihedral angles.
The plan includes merging L(G8) and L(G8d) into a single unified angular graph L(G8a) with a fixed feature size of (number of atoms x 64) to reduce computational complexity.
The plan includes the EGAT architecture with 10 stacked layers, edge-integrated node/edge attention, skip connections across layers, and a global attention pooling step.

The plan includes the compositional pipeline: mat2vec atomic embeddings passed through a ResNet to obtain a chemical embedding matrix, combined element-wise with a sinusoidal stoichiometric positional encoding to form the compositional matrix.
The plan includes concatenating positionally-embedded human-extracted physical/chemical descriptors to the compositional matrix to form the human-knowledge matrix.
The plan includes the CoTAN transformer module with 6 layers of 8-head multi-head attention blocks followed by a DenseNet to produce the compositional fingerprint.

The plan includes combining the structural fingerprint (from CrysGNN) and the compositional fingerprint (from CoTAN) and feeding them into a shared fully connected layer for the final property prediction.

The plan includes the training protocol: Adam optimizer with a look-ahead learning-rate schedule cycling between 1e-4 and 6e-3 every 4 epochs, MSE loss, and nested 5-fold cross-validation repeated 8 times with random weight initializations.

The plan includes the transfer-learning scheme (CrysCoT): pretraining parent models on Ef, Eg, or EHull, then reusing the atom-embedding/EGAT/transformer layers as a feature extractor while randomly re-initializing and retraining the later layers and a property-specific head at a small learning rate.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing all 8 benchmark datasets from MP21 (formation energy, band gap, phonons, dielectric refractive index, shear modulus, bulk modulus, energy above hull, Fermi energy) following the same benchmarking protocol as MatBench.
The plan includes obtaining or reimplementing the baseline models (MEGNet, CGCNN, SchNet, ALIGNN, Matformer, DeeperGATGNN) for a fair comparison against CrysCo and CrysGNN.
The plan includes the ablation study varying the number of EGAT layers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15) on the formation energy task to study the accuracy/computation trade-off.
The plan includes a CrysCo_3body ablation variant that excludes dihedral-angle (L(G8d)) information, for comparison against ALIGNN-D.
The plan includes the extrapolation evaluation protocol using k-fold forward cross-validation, holding out the top 10% of target values as a 'high-test' extrapolation set.

The plan includes the elemental attention-score analysis: weighting each element's average attention score (0.7) and the number of compounds containing it (0.3), and discarding elements with fewer than 1000 data points.
The plan includes applying a pretrained CrysCoT-Mp_eform model to predict elastic moduli for the approximately 113,000 MP compounds that are missing elastic data ('scarce dataset').
The code constructs the atomic graph G8 by connecting all atom pairs within an 8 Angstrom cutoff radius.
The code constructs the bond line graph L(G8), with bond-distance nodes and bond-angle edges.
The code constructs the dihedral graph L(G8d), encoding dihedral angles as edges.
The code merges L(G8) and L(G8d) into the unified angular graph L(G8a) with a fixed per-atom feature size of 64.
The code implements an Embedding layer that converts atom type (z) into feature vectors.
The code expands bond distances (d) into a Radial Bessel basis representation as proposed by Gasteiger et al.
The code implements the edge-integrated attention weight computation w_ij combining transformed node features (Wh*hi, Wh*hj) and transformed edge features (We*eij).
The code applies a softmax normalization over neighboring nodes to compute the node-level attention coefficients alpha_ij.

The code implements the LeakyReLU-activated, concatenation-based attention mechanism parameterized by a single-layer feed-forward weight vector a, applied to the concatenation of Wh*hi, Wh*hj, and We*eij.
The code computes the updated node representation h_i' as the activated, attention-weighted sum of transformed edge features over neighboring nodes.
The code implements the edge-level attention coefficients beta_ij used to aggregate adjacent edge features onto each node.
The code computes the aggregated edge feature e_i' on each node as a weighted sum of adjacent edge features using beta_ij.
The code implements the edge update via an MLP taking h_i, h_j, e_i', e_j', and e_ij as inputs, with non-shared weight matrices We and Wh per module.
The code applies the EGAT node module before the edge module within each layer, and stacks 10 EGAT layers by default for the optimized CrysCo model.
The code implements skip connections that concatenate features across layers (multi-scale merge strategy).
The code implements a global attention network over all nodes for the final feature update, following the DeeperGATGNN approach.
The code applies average pooling over atom (node) features after the final EGAT layer to obtain the structural fingerprint, then passes it through a fully connected layer.
The code retrieves mat2vec element embeddings from the atomic number for the compositional pipeline.
The code passes mat2vec embeddings through a ResNet architecture to produce the chemical embedding matrix.
The code computes the stoichiometric embedding matrix from elemental fractions using sine/cosine transformer-style positional encoding, following the CrabNet procedure.
The code element-wise sums the chemical embedding matrix and the stoichiometric embedding matrix to form the compositional matrix.
The code converts human-extracted physical/chemical descriptors into a positionally-embedded matrix and concatenates it with the compositional matrix to form the human-knowledge matrix.
The code implements the CoTAN transformer module with 6 layers of 8-head multi-head attention blocks that iteratively update the compositional matrix.
The code implements the DenseNet module that transforms the final transformer output into the compositional fingerprint.

The code concatenates/combines the structural fingerprint and the compositional fingerprint and feeds the result into a shared fully connected layer to predict the target material property (the hybrid CrysCo model).
The code supports training the standalone structure-only CrysGNN model (without the compositional branch) for direct comparison against the hybrid CrysCo model.
The code supports training the standalone composition-only CoTAN model (without the structural branch).
The code configures the Adam optimizer together with a look-ahead/1cycle learning-rate scheduler that cycles the learning rate between 1e-4 and 6e-3 every 4 epochs.
The code uses mean squared error (MSE) as the training loss function.
The code implements nested 5-fold cross-validation matching the MatBench benchmarking protocol, repeated with 8 random weight initializations for statistical robustness.
The code implements data loaders/preprocessing for all 8 MP21 benchmark property datasets listed in Table 1.
The code computes bulk (K) and shear (G) modulus targets on a log10 scale using the Voigt-Reuss-Hill (VRH) approximation.
The code implements or integrates the baseline models (MEGNet, CGCNN, SchNet, ALIGNN, Matformer, DeeperGATGNN) for direct comparison against CrysCo.

The code implements the transfer-learning feature extractor E(.) that reuses the pretrained atom-embedding, EGAT layers, and transformer layers from a parent model trained on a primary property (Ef, Eg, or EHull).
The code implements a property-specific head neural network H(.) on top of the feature extractor output for predicting the data-scarce downstream property.

The code implements the fine-tuning procedure for CrysCoT: randomly re-initializing the last few layers of the pretrained model while retaining and retraining the remaining layers at a small learning rate.
The code implements the CrysCo_3body ablation variant by disabling/removing the dihedral-angle graph L(G8d) from the angular graph construction.
The code supports configuring the number of stacked EGAT layers as a hyperparameter (e.g., 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15) to reproduce the layer-count ablation.

The code implements the forward cross-validation split that designates the top 10% of target values as the 'high-test' extrapolation set and the remaining 0-90% range for training/validation/'low-test'.

The code extracts per-element attention scores from the trained CrysGNN model and computes the weighted elemental contribution (0.7 x average attention + 0.3 x number-of-compounds), discarding elements with fewer than 1000 data points.

The code implements an inference pipeline that applies a pretrained CrysCoT-Mp_eform model to the ~113,000 MP compounds lacking elastic data and aggregates per-element average predicted moduli for binary/ternary oxide and non-oxide subsets.
The code supports running inference with the trained Ef/EHull models on an external/novel dataset of DFT-relaxed structures outside MP21.
The CrysCo hybrid model has been trained and evaluated via nested 5-fold cross-validation on all 8 MP21 benchmark datasets without errors.
The standalone CrysGNN model has been trained and evaluated on the same 8 benchmark datasets without errors.
The EGAT layer-count ablation (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15 layers) has been executed successfully on the formation-energy task, recording MAE and time-per-epoch for each configuration.
The CrysCo_3body ablation (without dihedral angles) has been trained and evaluated successfully for comparison against ALIGNN-D.
The transfer-learning pipeline has been executed successfully: parent models pretrained on Ef and EHull, then fine-tuned for phonons, dielectric, elasticity log10(G), and elasticity log10(K).
The extrapolation analysis (forward cross-validation with high-test/low-test split) has been executed successfully for shear and bulk moduli using the CrysCoT-Mp_eform model.
The dataset-size convergence experiments have been executed successfully across varying training set sizes for EHull, Ef, bulk modulus, and shear modulus.
The elemental-attention-score experiment has been executed successfully by training CrysGNN on the EHull dataset for oxide materials.
The elastic-data-filling inference has been executed successfully on the ~113,000-compound scarce dataset using the pretrained CrysCoT-Mp_eform model.
The trained Ef and EHull models have been run successfully on the novel 751-structure external dataset to produce predictions.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: CrysCo achieves the lowest or near-lowest MAE among the MEGNet, CGCNN, SchNet, ALIGNN, Matformer, and DeeperGATGNN baselines for the majority of the 8 properties, with Ef and EHull improved over MEGNet/CGCNN/SchNet/DeeperGATGNN by roughly 7-23%.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: the structure-only CrysGNN model shows a MAE at least ~3% higher than the full hybrid CrysCo model across properties.
The execution outcomes match the discussion of Table 2: Matformer attains slightly lower MAE than CrysCo only for the dielectric property (~0.6% lower), while CrysCo is equal or better elsewhere.
The execution outcomes match the discussion of Table 2: CrysCo outperforms ALIGNN on 7 of 8 datasets, with equivalent MAE on band gap and Fermi energy.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: MAE improves by roughly 40% when increasing EGAT layers from 2 to 10 (at ~2.6x compute cost), while going from 10 to 15 layers yields under 2% further improvement at a 31% time increase, justifying the choice of 10 layers.
The execution outcomes match Figure 2: CrysCo achieves higher accuracy than ALIGNN and Matformer at a comparable computation time of about 1.75-2 minutes per epoch.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: CrysCoT-Mp_eform and CrysCoT-Mp_ehull outperform the non-transfer-learning CrysCo baseline on phonons, dielectric, and elasticity log10(G)/log10(K), e.g. ~2.5% and ~1.7% improvement for shear and bulk moduli respectively.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3: prediction errors for the 'high-test' extrapolation set are comparable to the 'low-test' interpolation set for both shear modulus (MAE ~0.068 vs ~0.080) and bulk modulus (MAE difference < 0.0089).

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: CrysCoT-Mp_eform converges faster than non-transfer-learning models, reaching MAE below ~0.13 log(GPa) for both bulk and shear moduli with only 1000 training data points.

The execution outcomes match the novel-dataset evaluation: Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.894 (Ef) and 0.931 (EHull) and MAEs of 0.0318 and 0.0294 eV/atom respectively on the 751 new DFT-relaxed structures.

The execution outcomes match Figure 5: silicon and aluminum show among the highest elemental attention scores (associated with greater thermodynamic stability/lower EHull), while heavy alkali metals and halogens show comparatively lower scores.
The execution outcomes match Figure 6: predicted elastic moduli for the scarce (missing-data) compounds follow distribution patterns similar to the calculated training/test data.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: average per-element bulk and shear moduli for the scarce dataset show good Pearson linear correlation with calculated training-data averages for binary/ternary oxides and non-oxides, with the exception of binary-oxide shear modulus being noisier.
